title: Punctuation Basics
chapter: Chapter 2: Punctuation - The Traffic Signals of Writing

sections.heading: Why Punctuation Matters
sections.body: Punctuation controls how your reader reads. It tells them when to pause, stop, and feel emotion.

comparison.wrong: let's eat grandma let's eat grandma foods and recipes for eating grandma
comparison.right: Let's eat, Grandma! Let's eat, Grandma! Foods and recipes for eating, Grandma.

sections.key_point: Punctuation literally saves lives — and grandmas! Punctuation = Road signs for your reader.

sections.heading: The Period (.)
sections.explanation: A period (.) ends a complete thought. Also called a 'full stop' in British English.

uses.title: Ending a statement
examples: The sun rises in the east.
I finished my homework.
She works at a hospital.
Dogs are loyal animals.

uses.title: Abbreviations
examples: Dr. Smith and Prof. Jones met at approx. 3 p.m.

uses.title: Initials
examples: J.K. Rowling wrote Harry Potter.
The USA was founded in 1776.

common_mistakes.wrong: I love reading books I read every day
common_mistakes.right: I love reading books. I read every day.
I woke up at 7 a.m. (one period is enough)

sections.heading: The Comma (,)
sections.explanation: The comma (,) is the most used AND most misused punctuation mark in English.

uses.title: Lists (3 or more items)
examples: I bought apples, oranges, and bananas.
She is smart, kind, and hardworking.
We visited Paris, Rome, and London.
uses.note: The last comma before 'and' is called the Oxford Comma. It prevents confusion.

oxford_comma_example.without: I love my parents, Batman and Superman. (sounds like Batman and Superman ARE your parents!)
oxford_comma_example.with: I love my parents, Batman, and Superman. (clearly three separate things)

uses.title: Joining two complete sentences with FANBOYS
uses.explanation: FANBOYS = For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So (Coordinating Conjunctions)
examples: I wanted to go, but it was raining.
She studied hard, so she passed the exam.
He was tired, yet he kept working.
You can call me, or you can text me.
I worked all day, for I needed the money.
wrong_examples: I wanted to go but it was raining.
She studied hard so she passed the exam.

uses.title: After Introductory Words/Phrases
uses.explanation: When a sentence STARTS with extra info → use comma after that intro part
examples: However, I disagree with that idea.
In 2020, the pandemic changed everything.
After the movie, we went for dinner.
Although it was cold, we went outside.
First of all, let me introduce myself.
On the other hand, there are benefits.
To be honest, I don't know the answer.

uses.title: Setting off Extra Information
uses.explanation: When you add extra info in the MIDDLE of a sentence → put commas around it
examples: My teacher, Mr. Johnson, is very helpful.
London, the capital of England, is beautiful.
The dog, which was brown, ran away.
uses.note: If removing the middle part leaves the sentence complete → it needs commas

uses.title: In Dates and Addresses
examples: I was born on July 4, 1990, in New York.
The event is on Monday, January 20, 2025.
Send it to 123 Main Street, New York, USA.

common_mistakes.description: Comma splice (joining two sentences with ONLY a comma - no FANBOYS)
common_mistakes.wrong: I was tired, I went to sleep.
right_options: I was tired, so I went to sleep.
I was tired. I went to sleep.

common_mistakes.wrong: I tried hard but I failed.
common_mistakes.right: I tried hard, but I failed.

common_mistakes.wrong: However I think you are wrong.
common_mistakes.right: However, I think you are wrong.

sections.heading: The Question Mark (?)
sections.explanation: Use question mark at the end of a DIRECT question.

direct_questions.description: A direct question is when you DIRECTLY ask something.
examples: What is your name?
Where are you going?
Can you help me?
Why did you do that?
Is this seat taken?
How many people are coming?

indirect_questions.description: An indirect question is when you REPORT or INCLUDE a question inside a statement. Do NOT use ?

examples.wrong: She asked me where was I going?
examples.right: She asked me where I was going.

examples.wrong: I wonder what is his name?
examples.right: I wonder what his name is.

examples.wrong: He asked whether I could help him?
examples.right: He asked whether I could help him.

indirect_questions.memory_trick: If the sentence starts with 'I wonder...', 'He asked...', 'She questioned...' → It is INDIRECT → NO question mark!

sections.heading: The Exclamation Mark (!)
sections.explanation: Use exclamation mark to show strong emotion (surprise, excitement, anger), commands, or emphasis.

wrong_uses.wrong: I went to the store! It was nice! I bought milk! Then I came home! The milk was good!
wrong_uses.note: Save ! for truly exciting moments only. Too many = none feel special.

wrong_uses.right: My name is John. (your name is not exciting news)

sections.pro_tip: In professional/academic writing: Use exclamation mark RARELY (almost never). Maximum 1-2 times in an entire essay. Let strong words create emphasis instead.

sections.heading: The Apostrophe (')
sections.explanation: Apostrophes have TWO main jobs: 1. Show possession (belonging) and 2. Show missing letters (contractions).

examples: John's book (the book belongs to John)
the dog's tail (the tail of the dog)
my mother's car (the car of my mother)
the company's policy
today's weather

multiple_owners_s.rule: Multiple owners (plural ending in S) → add ' only
examples: the teachers' lounge (lounge for teachers)
the dogs' food bowls (bowls for dogs)
the students' books (books of students)
the workers' rights (rights of workers)

multiple_owners_no_s.rule: Multiple owners (plural NOT ending in S) → add 's
examples: the children's toys (toys of children)
the men's room (room for men)
the women's team (team of women)
the people's choice (choice of people)

biggest_mistake.title: ITS vs IT'S
rules: its = belonging to it (possession - NO apostrophe!)
wrong_examples: Its going to be a good day.
biggest_mistake.memory_trick: Replace with 'it is' in your sentence. If it makes sense → use it's. If it doesn't → use its.

tasks.instruction: Add correct punctuation (no changes to words) to each sentence.

sentences.text: its a lovely day isnt it
sentences.answer: It's a lovely day, isn't it?

sentences.text: however i disagree with your idea because the plan is not ready
sentences.answer: However, I disagree with your idea because the plan is not ready.

sentences.text: i bought rice vegetables oil and salt from the market but i forgot the bread
sentences.answer: I bought rice, vegetables, oil, and salt from the market, but I forgot the bread.

sentences.text: the dogs bowl is empty and its been empty since morning
sentences.answer: The dog's bowl is empty, and it's been empty since morning.

sentences.text: she asked me where was i going at that time
sentences.answer: She asked me where I was going at that time.

requirements: One sentence with a list of 4 items
One sentence joining two ideas with a FANBOY
One sentence with an apostrophe for possession
